title: Polymarket is America, America is Polymarket
gist: Polymarket's US trading volume quadrupled from $50m to $200m a day between May and June 2026 after regaining CFTC approval, but a Prospect analysis of its own market data and a string of insider-trading-adjacent scandals suggest its 'accuracy' claims are overstated and its ties to the Trump family create structural conflicts of interest.

insights.claim: Polymarket's US exchange trading volume rose fourfold, from $50m a day to $200m a day, between May and June 2026.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: A concrete, recent inflection in usage post-US-relaunch — the growth curve, not just the concept, is the tradeable signal.
insights.quote: The volume of trades made on its US exchange rose fourfold, from $50m a day to $200m, between May and June this year.
insights.locator: Post no bills

insights.claim: Polymarket was only allowed to return to the US in 2025 after gaining CFTC regulatory approval, and that return is what has driven its recent growth surge; its latest formal valuation in October 2025 was $9bn.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Regulatory approval, not organic demand alone, is the growth trigger — watch CFTC posture as the key swing factor for the whole prediction-market sector.
insights.locator: it was only in 2025 that it was allowed to return to the US

insights.claim: Polymarket raised $45m in a May 2024 round led by Peter Thiel's Founders Fund, before it was legally able to operate in the US.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Elite VC backing pre-dated regulatory clearance, signaling investors bet on the regulatory environment shifting favorably under a Trump administration.
insights.locator: including $45m in a May 2024 investment round

insights.claim: Donald Trump Jr has sat on Polymarket's advisory board since his firm, 1789 Capital, made a 'double-digit millions of dollars' investment in August 2025, and he is separately a 'strategic advisor' to competitor Kalshi.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: The same family figure sits on both leading platforms' boards, a direct conflict-of-interest structure that likely shaped the administration's favorable regulatory treatment of the sector.
insights.locator: Donald Trump Jr has sat on Polymarket's advisory board

insights.claim: Mark Zuckerberg has tasked Meta with building a prediction-markets app, provisionally named 'Arena', reviving an earlier abortive 2020 attempt called 'Forecast'; it would initially use a video-game-like points system rather than real money.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: A Meta entrant would bring prediction markets to a vastly larger user base and could commoditize the category or squeeze independent platforms' growth.
insights.quote: would probably rely on a video-game-like points system instead
insights.locator: In June, the New York Times reported

insights.claim: Almost half of Polymarket's current events relate to sports, raising unresolved state-by-state US gambling-regulation conflicts since the platform is accessible to anyone over 18 while sports betting is typically restricted to 21+.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: A material regulatory overhang: state-level gambling law could still constrain or reshape Polymarket's largest category.
insights.locator: how do they handle a platform that's dressed up

themes.theme: Prediction markets' academic and regulatory history

insights.claim: The Iowa Political Stock Market, launched by University of Iowa economists in the late 1980s to predict the 1988 presidential election, outperformed major pollsters including Gallup and Harris, and its successor, the Iowa Electronic Markets, has continued to do so in subsequent elections.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: The original academic case for prediction markets rests on real, decades-long outperformance versus polling — the underlying mechanism is not junk, even if today's platforms distort it.
insights.locator: The Iowa Political Stock Market outperformed major pollsters

insights.claim: DARPA's Policy Analysis Market (PAM), designed with economist Robin Hanson to let analysts bet on geopolitical events including assassinations and coups in Middle Eastern countries, was killed by Congress in 2003 after senators Byron Dorgan and Ron Wyden called it 'morally repugnant'.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Political backlash, not economic failure, has historically been the binding constraint on prediction markets for sensitive events — a recurring risk for today's platforms as they price assassinations and wars.
insights.locator: in 2003, Pam was strangled

insights.claim: Robin Hanson has developed the concept of 'futarchy' — governance that combines democratic voting on values with prediction-market forecasting on which policies best achieve them — and Polymarket founder Shayne Coplan was an early adherent who once wrote to Hanson expressing support.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Polymarket's founding ideology was explicitly utopian/governance-oriented, useful context for evaluating gaps between its stated mission and its current gambling-heavy reality.
insights.quote: could we give people more direct, better incentives to actually tell the truth and figure out the truth
insights.locator: In a podcast interview in 2021

insights.claim: Early 2000s prediction-market firms HedgeStreet and Intrade fell into regulatory limbo between financial-instrument and gambling law, and regulation tightened further after the 2008 financial crash, delaying the sector's mainstream emergence until 2020.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Regulatory clarity, more than technology, has been the gating factor for the category's growth — today's favorable US stance is the real unlock.
insights.locator: these early-moving firms often fell foul

insights.claim: Kalshi became the first major modern player by registering as a CFTC-regulated 'contract market' in 2020, while Polymarket launched the same year as an unregulated, blockchain-based alternative funded and built by 22-year-old founder Shayne Coplan.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Two structurally different entrants (regulated vs. crypto-native) both emerged in 2020, framing the current competitive duopoly and its differing regulatory exposure.
insights.locator: That year, one of two new big players

insights.claim: The CFTC fined Polymarket $1.4m in 2022 for running an unsanctioned trading platform and effectively banned it from the US until it achieved regulatory compliance, a ban that lasted until 2025.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: The platform has a track record of regulatory violation preceding its current favorable treatment — a reversal worth watching for durability risk.
insights.quote: All derivatives markets must operate within the bounds of the law regardless of the technology used
insights.locator: The CFTC fined Polymarket $1.4m in 2022

insights.claim: As of the article's writing, the Wall Street Journal reported a source saying the CFTC is 'in the midst of a continuing investigation into Polymarket', despite the platform's recent favorable regulatory treatment.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Regulatory risk has not fully disappeared even under a friendlier administration — an active investigation is a live tail risk for the platform.
insights.quote: in the midst of a continuing investigation into Polymarket
insights.locator: the CFTC may have got involved again

themes.theme: Accuracy claims don't hold up under scrutiny

insights.claim: Polymarket markets ask a binary yes/no question and price shares such that the price reflects the market's implied probability (e.g., a $0.95 'No' price implies a 95% chance); correct shares settle at $1, incorrect at $0.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Understanding the mechanics clarifies how to read Polymarket prices as real-time probability estimates, and where their limits lie.
insights.locator: All of its markets ask a binary, yes-or-no question

insights.claim: Polymarket claims its crowd-sourced predictions are accurate more than 94% of the time a month before an outcome is known, but this figure appears to trace to research measuring accuracy just four hours before market closure, when outcomes are typically already near-certain.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: The platform's headline accuracy statistic is likely a misleading marketing figure — investors citing Polymarket 'accuracy' should demand the actual measurement window.
insights.quote: accurate more than 94% of the time an entire month before an outcome is definitively known
insights.locator: Polymarket boasts that its crowd-sourced predictions

insights.claim: A Prospect analysis of almost 7,000 Polymarket markets from the past 18 months, each with more than $100,000 in trading volume, found markets already trading a month before closure called the outcome correctly only 81% of the time — well below the platform's own 94%+ claim.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: An independent, methodologically comparable benchmark (81% vs. claimed 94%+) is the concrete number to cite when discounting Polymarket's marketing claims.
insights.locator: A Prospect analysis of almost 7,000 markets

insights.claim: A week before the assassination of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on 28 February in a US-Israeli assault, a Polymarket market titled 'Khamenei out as Supreme Leader of Iran by February 28?' priced the probability at just 2%.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: A concrete high-profile miss undercuts the 'wisdom of crowds' narrative for tail-risk geopolitical events specifically — exactly the events investors most want forecast.
insights.quote: Khamenei out as Supreme Leader of Iran by February 28?
insights.locator: a week before the assassination of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei

insights.claim: A Polymarket market asking 'Will Jesus Christ return before 2027?' has had $64m in volume pushed through it, with the implied probability just over 2%, illustrating that large sums flow into markets with no informational basis.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Large trading volume is not proof of informational value — a caution against treating Polymarket price levels as inherently meaningful signal.
insights.locator: Among Polymarket's current markets is one

themes.theme: Insider information and market integrity risk

insights.claim: An anonymous French investor (the 'French whale') bet roughly $80m on Trump defeating Kamala Harris in the 2024 election, contrary to pollsters' 'toss-up' narrative, partly informed by data he had specially commissioned from YouGov, and won more than double his stake, an additional $85m.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Illustrates that large, privately-commissioned data edges — not just crowd wisdom — can drive Polymarket's most famous 'accurate' calls.
insights.locator: an anonymous investor from France

insights.claim: Nine linked Polymarket accounts won more than $2.4m betting on Trump's military interventions in Iran, with a 98% win rate across bets even when priced probabilities were much lower, which the CEO of the data analytics firm that discovered it said luck alone cannot explain.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Strong circumstantial evidence of information asymmetry or insider access on geopolitical/military-event markets — a live integrity risk for anyone trading alongside these flows.
insights.quote: Luck alone cannot explain those numbers
insights.locator: Nine Polymarket accounts thought to have one person

insights.claim: A US special forces soldier has been charged with (but denies) using classified information to win $400,000 betting on Nicolás Maduro's removal from office just hours before Maduro was captured by US special forces; an Israeli Air Force crew member told interrogators 'the entire squadron is on Polymarket, the entire air force is betting.'
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that military/intelligence personnel with privileged information are actively trading Polymarket on events they may influence or foreknow — a structural insider-trading exposure the platform has no mechanism to police.
insights.quote: The entire squadron is on Polymarket, the entire air force is betting.
insights.locator: An Israeli Air Force crew member who was questioned

insights.claim: Polymarket's resolution of the 'Will Zelenskyy wear a suit before July?' market — ruling that a black jacket-and-trousers outfit widely described as a suit by media and its own designer was not a suit under an outside blockchain dispute-resolution process — shifted millions of dollars based on a subjective judgment call.
insights.speaker: Peter Hoskin
insights.so_what: Shows resolution-source subjectivity is itself a trading risk on ambiguous real-world questions, independent of any informational edge.
insights.quote: Will Zelenskyy wear a suit before July?
insights.locator: Then there was the incident of Zelensky's suit
